briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon, everyone. We will now begin the climate briefing for March 8, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Worsening Global Climate Crisis and Its Impact on Vulnerable Populations
sections.content: According to recent research, the devastating impacts of climate change are more severe than anticipated, and the climate crisis is escalating globally [28]. The World Health Organization (WHO) has issued a climate change warning for the Horn of Africa region [37], and heritage sites like Thabo Monastery are also at risk due to climate change [36]. Reports indicate that women and girls in Africa are paying the highest price due to increasing debt, fossil fuel extraction, and climate change, with insufficient funding to address these issues [40, 41]. Furthermore, the WDR 2026 report has been released, stating that climate change is becoming a breeding ground for harmful information, alongside vaccine hesitancy and migration [32].

sections.title: ⚠️ Challenges in Food Security and Climate Science Research
sections.content: Climate change is affecting global coffee production [29], and news reports indicate a surge in food prices [33]. These food security issues can be interpreted as direct impacts of climate change. Meanwhile, budget cuts at Environment and Climate Change Canada are threatening Arctic science research [30], and concerns have been raised that some climate change data is providing inaccurate information for the Prairie region [39]. This poses significant challenges for establishing an accurate scientific basis for climate change response.

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic Renewable Energy Transition Efforts and Increased Investment
sections.content: Domestically, there is active positive momentum for expanding renewable energy. Minister Kim Sung-hwan and Han Jae-yeon formed a consensus on renewable energy expansion through a meeting [11], and Chungnam Governor Kim Tae-heum attracted 1 trillion KRW in renewable energy investment during his trip to Singapore [14]. In particular, Hana Bank and Korea Southern Power (KOSPO) have signed a memorandum of understanding (MOU) for the joint development of offshore wind power projects, accelerating the major transition to renewable energy [10, 12, 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 21]. Community-led 'rural solar power' projects have also entered a year of significant growth [13], and Yeonggwang County has declared itself an 'Energy Basic Income City,' sharing renewable energy profits with its residents, thereby encouraging local community participation [20].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Encouraging Local Community Participation in Carbon Neutrality Practices
sections.content: Various local governments in South Korea are promoting policies to encourage citizens' participation in carbon neutrality practices. Gwangju City and Busan City are recruiting vehicles to participate in the Automobile Carbon Neutrality Point System [4, 6, 7], and drivers who reduce their mileage will receive incentives of up to 100,000 KRW [4, 7]. These local-level efforts provide practical ways for citizens to contribute to carbon emission reduction in their daily lives and play a crucial role in the transition to a carbon-neutral society.

briefing.closing: In today's briefing, we examined the severity of the global climate crisis and the response efforts both domestically and internationally. Based on an understanding of the complex impacts of climate change, more proactive and integrated response strategies are anticipated to be necessary. This concludes today's climate briefing.
